proposals.title: ConvNeXt-Scale: Scaling Laws and Diffusion-Augmented Training for Modern Convolutional Networks
proposals.motivation: The ConvNeXt architecture has demonstrated that properly modernized CNNs can match or exceed Vision Transformers (ViTs) on standard benchmarks like ImageNet-1K. However, its scalability on truly large-scale datasets (e.g., ImageNet-21K, JFT) and in the billion-parameter regime remains an open question, especially compared to the established scaling laws for Transformers. Understanding these limits is crucial for determining the most efficient and performant foundation model architecture for vision.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ConvNeXt exhibits predictable scaling laws with dataset and model size that are competitive with ViTs, and that its training can be significantly enhanced on large datasets using targeted, diffusion-based data augmentation to improve sample efficiency and generalization.
proposals.method: We will conduct systematic scaling experiments with ConvNeXt, scaling model parameters from tens of millions to over a billion, and training on datasets from ImageNet-1K to ImageNet-21K and a large internal video/image collection. We will analyze performance scaling trends (compute-optimal frontiers) and architectural bottlenecks. Critically, inspired by recent diffusion-based augmentation work, we will develop a task-aware diffusion model to generate synthetic training data that specifically targets the under-represented or challenging visual concepts identified during ConvNeXt's training trajectory, integrating this augmentation dynamically into the training loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first comprehensive scaling laws for a modern pure CNN architecture, providing a critical empirical comparison to Transformer scaling. It will also deliver a novel, scalable training recipe for ConvNeXt that leverages generative models for efficient large-scale learning, potentially setting new state-of-the-art results for CNNs on major benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws are well-studied for Transformers, they are unexplored for modern CNNs like ConvNeXt. The integration of a guided, task-aware diffusion model for scalable dataset augmentation specifically to overcome scaling bottlenecks in a CNN's training is a novel synthesis of Darrell group themes.

proposals.title: Web-Scale Diffusion Adaptation: Scaling Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning to Billion-Image Datasets
proposals.motivation: Current parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods for large diffusion models, such as LoRA, are primarily validated on curated, medium-scale datasets like COCO or LAION subsets. However, the real-world potential of diffusion models lies in adapting to the immense scale and diversity of web-crawled image-text data. It is unknown whether these adaptation methods maintain stability, efficiency, and performance gains when scaled to billions of noisy and heterogeneous examples, a critical step for creating truly general-purpose visual generative models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating large language model (LLM)-based prompt refinement and a novel, curriculum-based dropout strategy for adapter modules, we can achieve stable and sample-efficient adaptation of text-to-image diffusion models on web-scale datasets, outperforming standard fine-tuning and existing PEFT methods in both quality and diversity.
proposals.method: We will scale the 'LLM-grounded Diffusion' framework to a multi-billion image dataset (e.g., a filtered subset of LAION-5B). The core adaptation will use LoRA modules applied to a foundational diffusion model (e.g., Stable Diffusion). A key innovation is a 'data-curriculum dropout' mechanism, where the probability of applying specific LoRA paths is dynamically adjusted based on the perceived complexity and noise level of each training batch, guided by an auxiliary LLM that re-captions and scores incoming data. Training will leverage a distributed pipeline to manage the computational load of joint LLM-diffusion processing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic study of large-scale adaptation for diffusion models, establishing best practices and a novel stable training algorithm for web-scale data. It will deliver a powerful, adapted model demonstrating superior prompt fidelity and output diversity, alongside insights into the scaling laws of diffusion model fine-tuning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses LLMs to guide diffusion or applies PEFT on small datasets, the novelty lies in the co-design of a scalable training system that uses an LLM not just for prompt understanding but also for real-time data curriculum scheduling, enabling robust learning from massive, noisy corpora.

proposals.title: Contrastive Action Feature Learning (CAFL): Self-Supervised Video Representation for Robust Action Prediction
proposals.motivation: Current video action prediction models rely heavily on large-scale, manually annotated datasets, which are costly and often biased. This limits their generalization to diverse, real-world scenarios where action semantics are complex and nuanced. A self-supervised approach that learns robust, temporally-aware feature representations from unlabeled video data is crucial for scalable and adaptable video understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a contrastive self-supervised learning objective, designed to maximize mutual information between temporally consistent video segments while discriminating against spurious correlations, will yield a feature space that significantly improves the robustness and sample efficiency of downstream action prediction models.
proposals.method: We will develop a dual-encoder architecture (e.g., a modified TimeSformer or 3D-ResNet) trained with a novel contrastive loss. Positive pairs will be generated by applying temporally consistent augmentations (e.g., frame dropout, mild temporal warping) to short video clips, inspired by recent work on diffusion-based data augmentation. Negative pairs will be formed from clips across different videos and from temporally distant segments within the same video. We will integrate a momentum encoder and a large memory bank to manage the negative sample dictionary efficiently. The pre-trained encoder will be fine-tuned for action prediction on downstream tasks like Epic-Kitchens or Something-Something V2.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, scalable framework for self-supervised video representation learning specifically optimized for action prediction. It will demonstrate improved robustness to distribution shifts and superior sample efficiency compared to supervised baselines. The resulting feature space will provide a strong, generic foundation for a range of video understanding tasks.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning has been applied to images and generic video clips, its specific application to learn features for *future* action prediction, using temporally-aware augmentations and negative sampling strategies that explicitly model action boundaries, is novel. This bridges the gap between self-supervised representation learning and the temporal causality required for prediction.

proposals.title: DETR-ive: Unsupervised Diffusion-Augmented Pre-training for Open-Vocabulary Object Detection
proposals.motivation: Object detection models rely heavily on supervised ImageNet pre-training, which is costly, label-intensive, and biases models towards a closed set of categories. This limits their adaptability to novel concepts and real-world open-vocabulary settings. Unsupervised feature learning offers a path to more scalable and flexible visual representations, but its effectiveness for complex downstream tasks like detection remains under-explored compared to classification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an unsupervised pre-training strategy, leveraging recent advances in diffusion models and language-grounded image synthesis, can learn visual features that match or surpass supervised ImageNet pre-training for object detection, particularly in data-scarce and open-vocabulary scenarios.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage pre-training framework. First, we will train a visual encoder using a self-supervised objective (e.g., DINO or iBOT) on a large, diverse corpus of images. Second, we will employ a diffusion-based augmentation model, guided by a Large Language Model (LLM) to generate semantically controlled variations of training images, focusing on object attributes, backgrounds, and viewpoints critical for detection. This augmented data will be used to further refine the encoder with a reconstruction or contrastive loss. The resulting features will be evaluated by fine-tuning a detection architecture like DETR or Mask R-CNN on COCO and LVIS, comparing against supervised ImageNet baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous benchmark for unsupervised pre-training in detection, moving beyond classification. It will deliver a robust, open-vocabulary-ready visual backbone that reduces dependency on manual annotation. Furthermore, it will demonstrate a novel synergy between self-supervised learning, LLM-guided diffusion models, and detection tasks.
proposals.novelty: While unsupervised pre-training and diffusion-based augmentation exist, their combined, targeted application to object detection is novel. The key innovation is using LLM-grounded diffusion to generate augmentations that explicitly reinforce detection-centric features like object boundaries and multi-instance scenes, rather than general image aesthetics.

proposals.title: Unified Video Object Understanding via Diffusion-Augmented, Language-Grounded One-Pass Networks
proposals.motivation: Current video understanding models often rely on complex, multi-stage pipelines for object classification and localization, which are computationally expensive and lack temporal coherence. A single-pass network that processes full video frames holistically is crucial for real-time applications like autonomous systems and interactive AI. This work addresses the efficiency and integration gap between video analysis, language grounding, and modern generative data augmentation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a transformer-based architecture, trained on video data augmented via language-guided diffusion models, can achieve state-of-the-art joint classification and localization in a single forward pass by leveraging unified spatiotemporal attention and cross-modal pretraining.
proposals.method: We will develop a Vision Transformer (ViT) variant that processes entire video clips as spatiotemporal patches. Inspired by LLM-grounded Diffusion, we will use a frozen large language model to generate descriptive text prompts for conditioning a diffusion model, creating semantically rich, varied video frame augmentations for training. The core network will integrate a DETR-like object detection head atop the transformer backbone, trained end-to-end with a combination of classification and bipartite matching localization losses, utilizing techniques like dropout for robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, efficient architecture for unified video understanding, eliminating the need for separate proposal networks. It will demonstrate a new paradigm for using language-guided diffusion as a powerful, semantic data augmentation engine for video tasks. The resulting model will provide a strong baseline for real-time, holistic scene comprehension in dynamic environments.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the synergistic integration of language-grounded diffusion for video data augmentation with a one-pass, transformer-based detection architecture, moving beyond image-based applications. This approach uniquely combines Darrell group themes of diffusion, language, and video learning into a single, end-to-end trainable system for spatiotemporal understanding.

proposals.title: Diffusion-Augmented End-to-End Learning with Margin-Maximizing Classifiers
proposals.motivation: While deep CNNs are typically trained with a softmax cross-entropy loss, the theoretical and practical benefits of margin-maximizing classifiers like SVMs are well-established, offering better generalization and robustness. However, a persistent gap exists between learning powerful CNN features and training a separate SVM on top, preventing true end-to-end optimization of the joint feature-classifier system. This limits the potential for creating compact, highly discriminative models, especially in low-data regimes common in video understanding tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an end-to-end framework that jointly optimizes CNN features with an integrated, differentiable SVM-like loss will yield more robust and generalizable representations than standard softmax training, particularly when augmented with synthetic data from diffusion models to enforce margin consistency.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel training framework where a CNN backbone is coupled with a final linear layer trained with a differentiable, hinge-loss-inspired objective that directly maximizes classification margin. To stabilize training and improve generalization, we will integrate a diffusion-based augmentation pipeline (inspired by Darrell's prior work) to generate challenging, on-the-fly synthetic examples. These will be used in a multi-task setup: one loss for real data and a consistency loss on augmented data to enforce a large, invariant margin. Optimization will use a modified gradient descent step that handles the non-smooth loss, potentially employing a sub-gradient approach or a smoothed surrogate.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, practical end-to-end training paradigm that unifies representation and max-margin classifier learning. It will provide empirical evidence on video and image benchmarks showing improved robustness and sample efficiency over standard softmax models. The integration of diffusion-based augmentation as a margin regularizer will also advance the use of generative models for discriminative task guidance.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the truly joint, end-to-end optimization of features and a margin-maximizing classifier within a modern deep learning framework, moving beyond the standard two-stage 'CNN features + SVM' pipeline. Furthermore, leveraging diffusion models not just for data diversity but explicitly for margin enforcement is a novel application of generative augmentation.

proposals.title: Temporally-Consistent R-CNN: A Diffusion-Augmented Framework for Video Object Detection
proposals.motivation: Object detection in video remains a significant challenge due to temporal inconsistencies, occlusions, and motion blur, which degrade frame-by-frame application of static detectors like R-CNN. Pre-2026, while video understanding is advancing, methods often rely on complex, end-to-end trainable architectures that lack the modularity and data efficiency of the proven R-CNN paradigm. This project addresses the need for a robust, adaptable detection framework that leverages temporal context without sacrificing the strengths of region-based recognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the core R-CNN principle of region proposal followed by region-specific feature extraction and classification can be effectively extended to video by integrating temporal consistency modules and that diffusion-based data augmentation can synthesize critical, rare temporal occlusion scenarios to improve robustness.
proposals.method: We will extend Faster R-CNN by first generating object proposals per frame using a Region Proposal Network (RPN). A novel Temporal RoI Pooling module will then aggregate features from a short clip centered on each proposal, using a lightweight transformer or 3D convolution to model temporal context for classification and bounding box regression. Inspired by Darrell's recent work, we will employ a controlled diffusion model to generate synthetic training sequences featuring objects under severe occlusion and motion blur, using text prompts to guide the creation of these challenging cases, thereby augmenting the dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) A modular, temporally-aware R-CNN variant for video object detection that maintains interpretability, 2) A novel methodology for using text-guided diffusion models to generate targeted video data augmentations for improving temporal robustness, and 3) New state-of-the-art results on standard video detection benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet VID).
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic integration of the classic, well-understood R-CNN framework with modern temporal modeling and generative data augmentation, specifically using diffusion models to create targeted temporal hard cases, rather than designing a wholly new video architecture from scratch.

proposals.title: Diffusion-Guided Feature Disentanglement for Interpretable Object Detection
proposals.motivation: While CNNs are the backbone of modern object detectors, the semantic meaning of their hierarchical features remains opaque, limiting our ability to diagnose failures and improve model robustness. Recent diffusion models have shown remarkable ability to generate and manipulate images based on semantic concepts. This work proposes to leverage these generative models as a novel probe to understand and visualize what detection-specific features represent at different network depths.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the feature maps of a CNN-based object detector (e.g., Faster R-CNN, DETR) can be semantically disentangled and visualized by using a text-guided diffusion model as a differentiable feature inverter, and that this process will reveal systematic biases and failure modes tied to specific hierarchical representations.
proposals.method: We will train a latent diffusion model (e.g., Stable Diffusion) conditioned on feature maps extracted from a frozen, pre-trained detector. Using a method inspired by LLM-grounded Diffusion, we will employ a large language model to generate diverse, structured text descriptions for feature manipulation. By performing gradient-based optimization in the diffusion sampling loop, we will synthesize input images that maximally activate specific detector features at various layers (e.g., RPN features, RoI-pooled features). We will analyze these synthesized patterns to categorize features by semantic role (e.g., texture, part, context, full object).
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first framework for semantically interpreting hierarchical features in object detection using generative models. We will contribute a diagnostic toolkit to visualize feature failures and a novel, diffusion-based data augmentation strategy that directly targets weak feature representations, potentially improving detector robustness on long-tail categories.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work using GANs for CNN feature visualization, we leverage the superior editability and text-guidance of diffusion models. Furthermore, we uniquely apply this to the structured, multi-task feature spaces of object detectors, not just classification networks.

proposals.title: DiffAug-MT: Multi-Task Detection and Segmentation with Diffusion-Based Task-Aware Augmentation
proposals.motivation: Current object detection and segmentation models are often trained separately or with simple multi-task heads on static datasets, leading to sub-optimal generalization and data inefficiency. Pre-2026, vision models struggle with robustness to diverse visual contexts, and while diffusion models have emerged as powerful data generators, their use in multi-task learning remains underexplored. This work addresses the need for training frameworks that leverage structured, task-aware synthetic data to improve joint representation learning for dense prediction tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-task learning framework for joint detection and segmentation, augmented with task-conditioned diffusion models, will learn more robust and generalizable visual representations than single-task or standard multi-task baselines, leading to superior performance on both tasks with less real data.
proposals.method: We propose a unified encoder-decoder architecture with shared backbone features and task-specific heads for bounding box regression and mask prediction. The core innovation is a training loop integrated with a diffusion-based augmentation pipeline, inspired by Darrell's prior work. For each batch, a large language model (LLM) will generate textual descriptions of novel scene compositions and object interactions, which then guide a text-to-image diffusion model to synthesize images. Crucially, we will use prompt engineering and attention maps from the diffusion model to automatically generate pseudo-labels (boxes and segmentation masks) for these synthetic images, creating a dynamic, task-aware augmentation stream. This synthetic data will be mixed with real data, applying gradient dropout and step-wise curriculum learning to balance task gradients.
proposals.contribution: This research will deliver: 1) a novel multi-task training framework that synergistically combines detection and segmentation with generative augmentation, 2) a method for automatic, task-grounded label generation from diffusion models, reducing reliance on manual annotation, and 3) empirical evidence showing improved data efficiency and cross-task robustness on benchmarks like COCO and LVIS.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning and diffusion augmentation exist separately, their tight integration for dense prediction tasks is novel. The approach uniquely uses the generative process to create semantically consistent labels for both tasks simultaneously, moving beyond simple image-level augmentation to holistic, task-driven data synthesis.

proposals.title: Meta-DiffAug: Few-Step Domain Adaptation via Meta-Learned Invariant Features and Diffusion-Based Augmentation
proposals.motivation: Deep vision models struggle with domain shift, requiring extensive labeled data for adaptation. While diffusion models offer powerful data augmentation, and meta-learning enables fast adaptation, current methods treat these approaches separately. A unified framework is needed to meta-learn a feature extractor that is inherently robust to domain shifts, using controllable, semantically-rich augmentations to simulate diverse training domains.
proposals.hypothesis: A feature extractor, meta-trained across a distribution of domains synthesized by a controllable diffusion model, will learn domain-invariant representations that enable rapid adaptation to novel, real-world domains with very few gradient steps.
proposals.method: We propose a model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) framework where the 'tasks' are different visual domains. The outer-loop meta-training domains are generated by a text-to-image diffusion model (e.g., Stable Diffusion), guided by a Large Language Model (LLM) to produce systematic variations (e.g., 'weather', 'style', 'time of day') as in 'LLM-grounded Diffusion'. The inner-loop adaptation is simulated by applying these augmentations with a learnable scheduling policy. The core feature extractor (e.g., a ViT) is meta-trained to minimize a combined loss of task performance and a domain-invariance metric (e.g., MMD) across these synthetic domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel meta-learning paradigm where the task distribution is generated and controlled by a diffusion model, 2) a feature extractor with strong out-of-the-box domain generalization and few-step adaptation capability, and 3) empirical validation on challenging cross-domain video and image benchmarks, demonstrating improved sample efficiency over standard fine-tuning and augmentation baselines.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight integration of LLM-guided diffusion as a *meta-training data engine* for domain generalization, moving beyond using diffusion for simple offline dataset expansion. This creates a closed-loop system where the meta-learning objective directly shapes the feature space to be invariant to the very transformations the diffusion model can produce.

proposals.title: LLM-Grounded Self-Training: Leveraging Diffusion and Language Models for Unsupervised Video Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Current state-of-the-art video understanding models are heavily reliant on large-scale, manually annotated datasets, which are expensive and time-consuming to create. This bottleneck limits scalability and adaptation to new domains. A framework that effectively leverages the vast amounts of unlabeled video data available, guided by semantic knowledge, is crucial for advancing video AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a self-training framework, where a video model's own predictions are refined and expanded using a Large Language Model (LLM) and a text-to-image/video diffusion model, can generate high-quality pseudo-labels and synthetic variations. This will enable effective learning from unlabeled video sequences, rivaling supervised performance.
proposals.method: We will develop a cyclical self-training framework. First, an initial video encoder (e.g., a TimeSformer) trained on minimal labeled data generates initial pseudo-labels for unlabeled clips. An LLM (e.g., GPT-4) will be prompted to reason about these clips and generate diverse, contextually consistent textual descriptions and potential action labels. These text prompts will then guide a video diffusion model (extending models like Video LDM) to synthesize plausible augmented video variants. The model will be re-trained on a mixture of original clips, their LLM-refined pseudo-labels, and the synthesized variants, using techniques like dropout for robustness. The cycle repeats, progressively improving the pseudo-label quality and model performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, end-to-end framework for unsupervised and semi-supervised video representation learning that tightly integrates vision, language, and generative models. It will provide a practical method to reduce annotation dependence and demonstrate new synergies between discriminative and generative AI for understanding dynamic scenes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses diffusion for image augmentation or LLMs for prompt engineering, our proposal uniquely integrates an LLM as a semantic reasoning engine *within* a self-training loop for video, using it to direct both pseudo-label refinement and diffusion-based video synthesis to combat confirmation bias.

proposals.title: Multi-Modal, Multi-Task Diffusion: A Unified Framework for Joint Video-Language Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Current state-of-the-art vision-language models, including diffusion-based approaches, are typically trained for a single primary task (e.g., text-to-image generation) or via sequential fine-tuning, leading to task interference and suboptimal use of multi-modal data. As models scale, the computational cost of training separate expert models for video understanding, language grounding, and conditional generation becomes prohibitive. A unified training paradigm is needed to efficiently learn shared, robust representations across modalities and tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, centrally-executed training framework that jointly optimizes for video prediction, language-conditioned image synthesis, and cross-modal retrieval through a shared diffusion-based backbone will yield superior performance and data efficiency compared to independently trained specialist models, by leveraging synergistic gradients across tasks.
proposals.method: We propose a transformer-based diffusion architecture with modality-specific encoders and a shared latent denoising U-Net. The framework will employ a dynamic task router and a gradient modulation layer, inspired by multi-task learning and dropout techniques, to manage conflicting gradients during joint training on datasets like Something-Something (video) and COCO (image-text). Training will alternate between denoising steps for pixel reconstruction (video frames, images) and contrastive loss steps for language alignment, with a scheduled sampling curriculum to balance task difficulty.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical, scalable framework for joint multi-modal training, reducing total compute by consolidating model footprints. It will provide empirical evidence on the synergies between generative and discriminative objectives within diffusion models. The resulting unified model will set a new benchmark for integrated video-language understanding and generation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses diffusion for augmentation or LLM-guided prompting, we natively integrate multiple core tasks (video prediction, text-to-image, retrieval) into a single end-to-end training loop of a diffusion model, challenging the single-model-single-task paradigm. The proposed gradient modulation mechanism for diffusion training is a novel solution to multi-task optimization in this context.

proposals.title: Recurrent Diffusion: A Unified Framework for Temporal-Consistent Video Generation and Augmentation
proposals.motivation: Current diffusion-based video generation and augmentation frameworks, including those from Darrell's group, are primarily built on convolutional or transformer-based U-Nets, which excel at spatial modeling but can struggle with explicit, long-range temporal coherence. Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), particularly LSTMs and GRUs, offer a strong, established inductive bias for sequential data but are not natively supported in modern diffusion backbones, limiting their potential for tasks requiring robust temporal dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating recurrent neural network layers into the core architecture of a diffusion model will significantly improve its ability to model long-term temporal dependencies in video, leading to more coherent generated sequences and more effective temporal augmentations for video training data.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel diffusion model architecture that interleaves convolutional spatial blocks with recurrent temporal blocks within the U-Net's encoder and decoder. Specifically, we will replace standard 3D convolutions in the temporal dimension with ConvLSTM or ConvGRU layers at multiple scales. The training will utilize a hybrid objective combining standard diffusion denoising loss with a temporal consistency loss enforced on latent feature trajectories. We will evaluate on video prediction, unconditional video generation, and diffusion-based video augmentation for action recognition datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, efficient architectural primitive for diffusion models that explicitly captures temporal structure, advancing the state-of-the-art in video generation quality. It will also extend the applicability of automatic diffusion-based augmentation to sequential data domains beyond static images, enabling better video model training with limited data.
proposals.novelty: While RNNs are classic for sequences, their integration as a core, differentiable component within a modern diffusion U-Net for end-to-end temporal modeling in video generation is unexplored. This moves beyond using RNNs merely as post-hoc refiners or separate temporal encoders.

proposals.title: Single-Image Diffusion: Task-Specific Adaptation via LLM-Grounded Augmentation and Dropout Regularization
proposals.motivation: Current text-to-image diffusion models require massive datasets for general pre-training but struggle to adapt to novel, user-specific tasks defined by a single example image. This limitation hinders personalized AI applications and few-shot learning in domains like specialized medical imaging or custom artistic style transfer, where data collection is prohibitive. Adapting large models with minimal data is a critical unsolved challenge in efficient visual learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a diffusion model can be effectively adapted to a new, user-defined visual task from a single image by leveraging a large language model (LLM) to generate a rich, task-aligned textual description and augmentation strategy, coupled with aggressive regularization to prevent catastrophic forgetting of the base model's knowledge.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained text-to-image diffusion model (e.g., Stable Diffusion) using a single target image and a user's natural language task prompt. First, an LLM (e.g., GPT-4) will ground the task by generating: 1) a dense, structured textual description of the image's content and style, and 2) a set of instructions for a diffusion-based augmenter to create a small, semantically consistent synthetic dataset. Second, we will fine-tune the diffusion model on this augmented set using a novel multi-step dropout schedule, randomly dropping cross-attention layers to the original text encoder to force reliance on the new LLM-grounded description and prevent overfitting. Training will use a reconstruction loss on the target image and its augmentations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for single-image, task-specific diffusion model adaptation, demonstrating that LLM-guided understanding and generation can effectively substitute for large datasets. It will provide new insights into regularization techniques for extreme low-data fine-tuning of large generative models, moving beyond traditional few-shot learning benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop integration of an LLM as a task interpreter and augmentation designer specifically for single-image diffusion training, combined with a tailored dropout mechanism applied to the text-conditioning pathway to maintain base model utility while adapting to a new concept.

proposals.title: Diffusion-Augmented Training of Fully Convolutional Networks for Segmentation from Scratch
proposals.motivation: Fully Convolutional Networks (FCNs) for semantic segmentation are almost universally initialized with ImageNet-pretrained classification backbones, creating a dependency on a specific, potentially biased, and task-disjoint dataset. This reliance limits architectural flexibility and raises concerns about the generalizability of learned features. A method to train performant segmentation models from random initialization would enable more robust and specialized architectures, free from upstream dataset constraints.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a training regimen combining task-specific synthetic data from diffusion models with a carefully designed, language-guided curriculum can enable FCNs to learn effective segmentation features from scratch, matching or exceeding the performance of ImageNet-initialized models.
proposals.method: We will train FCNs (e.g., DeepLabv3+ variants) from random initialization. The core method involves a two-stage process: 1) **Diffusion-Based Task Augmentation**: Using a text-to-image diffusion model (e.g., Stable Diffusion), guided by large language models (LLMs) to generate diverse, task-relevant images and their corresponding segmentation masks via prompt engineering and attention map extraction. 2) **Curriculum Learning with Language Guidance**: A training curriculum will be designed where the model first learns on simpler, LLM-generated synthetic scenes with clear object boundaries, progressively moving to more complex, real data. Techniques like scheduled sampling and adaptive dropout will be used to stabilize training from scratch.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a viable pipeline for training segmentation networks without ImageNet pre-training, decoupling segmentation research from classification backbones. It will provide insights into the role of synthetic, language-grounded data in learning low- and mid-level visual features. The resulting models will be evaluated for robustness and domain generalization against standard pre-trained baselines.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models are used for data augmentation, their systematic integration with LLM guidance to create a full training curriculum for *from-scratch* segmentation learning is novel. This approach directly leverages Darrell's recent work on LLM-grounded diffusion and automatic diffusion-based augmentation for a new, fundamental task.

proposals.title: Curriculum-Gated Learning: Stabilizing Joint FCN-CRF Training for Video Semantic Segmentation
proposals.motivation: Fully Convolutional Networks (FCNs) combined with Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) offer a powerful framework for structured prediction tasks like semantic segmentation, where global consistency is crucial. However, naive joint training is notoriously unstable; the CRF's structured loss often overwhelms or is ignored by the FCN, leading to divergence or suboptimal performance. This instability has prevented the reliable integration of powerful, modern deep learning backbones with structured probabilistic models, limiting progress in video understanding and other dense prediction domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a curriculum learning strategy, coupled with a gradient gating mechanism that selectively allows the structured loss to influence the FCN's feature learning, will stabilize joint training and yield a model that consistently outperforms its separately trained or naively joint-trained counterparts.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-phase curriculum. First, the FCN backbone (e.g., a Video Swin Transformer) is trained solely on per-pixel cross-entropy loss. Second, a CRF (or a differentiable, efficient approximation like a Gaussian CRF) is introduced. The core innovation is a gradient gating module that sits between the CRF's loss and the FCN. This gate, implemented via a small, learned network that processes the FCN's current feature statistics and the CRF loss gradient, outputs a soft mask to element-wise modulate the backpropagated gradient from the CRF. This prevents harmful updates while preserving beneficial ones, akin to an adaptive, learned form of gradient clipping or dropout specific to the structured loss stream.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) A robust, reproducible training framework for joint FCN-CRF models that overcomes a key technical barrier. 2) State-of-the-art results on challenging video semantic segmentation benchmarks (e.g., Cityscapes, VIPER) by effectively leveraging temporal consistency. 3) A generalizable gradient gating principle applicable to other unstable multi-loss training scenarios in deep learning.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning and gradient manipulation are known, their specific combination as a learned, adaptive gate to stabilize the notoriously difficult FCN-CRF joint training loop is novel. This moves beyond fixed scheduling or simple weighting to a dynamic, context-aware modulation of the learning signal.

proposals.title: Diffusion-CRF: End-to-End Joint Training of Diffusion Models and Conditional Random Fields for Structured Visual Prediction
proposals.motivation: Fully Convolutional Networks (FCNs) excel at per-pixel prediction but often produce outputs lacking spatial coherence and structural consistency. Pre-2026, combining FCNs with Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) as a post-processing step is common, but this disjoint training limits the model's ability to learn features optimal for the joint task. An end-to-end framework that integrates deep feature learning with structured graphical models is a critical, unsolved challenge for dense prediction tasks like video segmentation and scene understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly training a diffusion-based feature backbone with a CRF or factor graph model in a single, end-to-end differentiable pipeline will yield more spatially coherent and semantically accurate predictions than sequential or purely feedforward approaches, by allowing the feature extractor to learn representations explicitly optimized for the structured output space.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel architecture where a diffusion model denoiser (e.g., a U-Net) serves as the unary potential generator within a dense CRF. The pairwise potentials will be implemented via differentiable, Gaussian kernels operating on the diffusion model's intermediate feature maps. Crucially, we will employ a differentiable surrogate for the CRF inference step, such as a mean-field iteration unrolled as a recurrent neural network, enabling gradient flow through the entire system. This framework will be trained end-to-end using a combined loss of task-specific supervision (e.g., cross-entropy) and a regularization term from the CRF energy.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) A novel, fully differentiable architecture unifying diffusion-based representation learning with probabilistic graphical models. 2) Demonstrated state-of-the-art performance on tasks requiring structured output, such as semantic segmentation in video and complex scene generation, by enforcing spatial-temporal consistency. 3) A new training paradigm that bridges the gap between deep generative models and structured prediction.
proposals.novelty: While post-hoc CRFs are standard, and some works have attempted end-to-end training with simple pairwise terms, integrating a *diffusion model* as the core feature learner within a jointly trained CRF framework is novel. This leverages the rich, iterative denoising process of diffusion to explicitly model the structured dependencies that CRFs formalize, moving beyond simple convolutional backbones.

proposals.title: Instance-Conditioned Dynamic Gating for Robust Multi-Scale Feature Fusion in Diffusion Models
proposals.motivation: Current text-to-image diffusion models rely on fixed, pre-defined architectures (e.g., U-Net skip connections) for fusing multi-scale features. This static fusion strategy is suboptimal for handling the vast diversity of concepts and compositions in open-world prompts, as it cannot adapt to which feature scales are most semantically relevant for a given input instance. An input-adaptive fusion mechanism is needed to improve robustness on ambiguous or complex prompts, a known challenge in pre-2026 diffusion models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that conditioning multi-scale feature fusion on the input instance via a lightweight, auxiliary network will yield more semantically coherent and robust image generation than static fusion, particularly for prompts containing multiple objects or complex spatial relationships.
proposals.method: We will integrate a small, efficient gating network (e.g., a lightweight ConvNet or Transformer) into the decoder of a pre-trained diffusion model (e.g., Stable Diffusion). This network will take downsampled skip features from multiple U-Net scales as input and output per-location, per-channel soft gating weights. These dynamic weights will modulate the fusion of features in the main decoder pathway. The gating network will be trained via a distillation loss from a large language model (LLM) grounded image-text scorer (extending 'LLM-grounded Diffusion') to align fusion decisions with high-level prompt understanding, while the main diffusion model remains frozen to preserve prior knowledge.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, plug-in module for making feature fusion in diffusion models input-adaptive, demonstrably improving robustness on heterogeneous and ambiguous generation tasks. It will provide a new perspective on integrating lightweight, conditional computation into large generative models without full retraining.
proposals.novelty: While dynamic routing and gating exist in classification networks, applying instance-conditioned, spatially-varying gating to the feature fusion process of a frozen, large-scale diffusion model for improved prompt fidelity is novel. It merges ideas from efficient architectures and LLM-guided training specifically for generative model robustness.

proposals.title: Fully Convolutional Spatiotemporal Networks: A Unified Framework for Video Segmentation
proposals.motivation: Video understanding requires models that can efficiently process spatiotemporal data for tasks like segmentation and action localization. Current approaches often rely on 3D convolutions or two-stream architectures, which are computationally heavy and can struggle with long-range temporal dependencies. A fully convolutional network (FCN) framework extended to video offers a promising path toward efficient, end-to-end learning for dense spatiotemporal prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a fully convolutional architecture, extended with novel temporal aggregation modules and trained with a curriculum on large-scale, diffusion-augmented video data, can achieve state-of-the-art performance in video semantic segmentation while being more parameter- and compute-efficient than existing 3D convolutional models.
proposals.method: We will design a Fully Convolutional Spatiotemporal Network (FCSTN) by extending 2D FCN backbones with lightweight, parallel temporal processing pathways. These will integrate temporal shift modules and axial attention to capture motion. Training will leverage a curriculum learning strategy, starting with static frames and progressively introducing temporal complexity. We will apply automatic diffusion-based video augmentation, guided by large language models (as in 'LLM-grounded Diffusion'), to synthesize diverse training scenarios and improve model robustness, mitigating temporal overfitting via targeted dropout in the temporal pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, efficient architectural paradigm for dense video prediction, demonstrating superior performance on benchmarks like Cityscapes-VPS and YouTube-VOS. It will also provide a methodology for generating and leveraging semantically guided, temporally consistent synthetic video data for training, addressing data scarcity in video domains.
proposals.novelty: While FCNs are established for images, their direct extension to video with integrated, efficient temporal modeling and LLM-guided diffusion augmentation for spatiotemporal data is novel. This approach unifies efficient architecture design with data-centric training strategies specifically for video.

proposals.title: FCN-Diff: A Unified Framework for Dense Prediction via Language-Guided Diffusion Priors
proposals.motivation: Fully Convolutional Networks (FCNs) are foundational for dense prediction but often require large, task-specific datasets and struggle with generalization. Recent advances in diffusion models and language-vision alignment offer powerful generative priors and semantic grounding, yet they are primarily used for image synthesis, not for structured, pixel-wise regression tasks like depth estimation or keypoint prediction. A method that leverages these generative priors to enhance FCNs could improve data efficiency and cross-task generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a pre-trained text-to-image diffusion model as a learned, language-conditioned prior into an FCN-based multi-task architecture will significantly improve performance and data efficiency on diverse dense prediction tasks by providing strong, semantically-aware regularization.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stream architecture: a standard FCN encoder-decoder for dense prediction, and a frozen, pre-trained latent diffusion model (e.g., Stable Diffusion) serving as a prior network. The FCN's intermediate features will be aligned with the diffusion model's denoising UNet features via cross-attention and adaptive layer normalization (AdaLN), conditioned on natural language task descriptions (e.g., 'predict depth map'). Training will use a composite loss: task-specific regression loss (e.g., L1 for depth) plus a diffusion feature matching loss (LPIPS on features) and a novel 'denoising dropout' step where the FCN must predict clean features from noised inputs, guided by the language prompt.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, unified framework for dense prediction that systematically leverages generative diffusion priors. It will demonstrate improved accuracy and robustness, particularly in low-data regimes, across tasks like depth estimation, human keypoint prediction, and image denoising. The proposed 'denoising dropout' and language-conditioned feature alignment will offer new techniques for integrating discriminative and generative models.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models are used for data augmentation and image generation, using a frozen diffusion model as a direct, trainable prior network within an FCN for structured regression tasks is unexplored. The explicit language-based conditioning for task specification moves beyond simple multi-task learning towards a more general, promptable dense prediction system.

proposals.title: AMR-Net: Attention-Guided Multi-Scale Refinement for Learned Upsampling in Diffusion Models
proposals.motivation: Learned upsampling in generative vision models, such as diffusion models, often uses fixed or uniformly applied deconvolution layers, which inefficiently allocate parameters and compute across all spatial regions. This leads to either blurry reconstructions in complex areas or excessive computation for simple regions. Adapting principles from Computational Fluid Dynamics, where Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR) dynamically focuses resources, offers a promising path to more efficient and detailed image synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an iterative, attention-guided upsampling process, which dynamically refines feature maps only in regions of high perceptual importance, will produce higher-fidelity image details than uniform learned upsampling, while maintaining or improving parameter efficiency.
proposals.method: We will replace standard upsampling blocks in a diffusion model (e.g., Stable Diffusion's decoder) with a novel AMR-Inspired Refinement Module. This module uses a lightweight convolutional network to predict a spatial importance mask from low-resolution features, highlighting edges and textured regions. A trainable deconvolution layer's output is then element-wise multiplied by this mask, focusing its effect. The refined output can be iteratively passed through subsequent, similar modules, creating a multi-scale refinement cascade. The entire system will be trained end-to-end on image reconstruction and text-to-image generation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, efficient upsampling primitive for generative vision models that improves fine-detail synthesis. It will demonstrate a novel cross-disciplinary analogy, translating AMR concepts into a deep learning framework. The resulting method is expected to advance the state-of-the-art in high-resolution image generation and video prediction tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit translation of the AMR principle from CFD to neural upsampling, moving beyond static or attention-based upsampling by integrating a trainable, iterative, and spatially-adaptive refinement process directly into the deconvolution operation.

proposals.title: Spatially-Grounded Hierarchical Attention for Robust Diffusion-Based Video Generation
proposals.motivation: Current text-to-video diffusion models often struggle with spatial coherence and temporal consistency, as their attention mechanisms treat image patches as a flat sequence, neglecting inherent multi-scale spatial hierarchies. This leads to artifacts, object deformations, and unstable dynamics during generation, limiting their reliability for complex, long-form video synthesis. A principled architecture for fusing features across scales while respecting spatial structure is needed to improve robustness and fidelity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchically structured cross-layer attention mechanism, explicitly conditioned on learned spatial priors and modulated by global context, will significantly improve the spatial coherence, temporal stability, and adversarial robustness of diffusion-based video generation models compared to standard sequence-based attention.
proposals.method: We will integrate the proposed Hierarchically Structured Cross-Layer Attention (HSCA) module into a latent video diffusion backbone (e.g., based on Stable Video Diffusion). The module will contain two parallel attention pathways: intra-scale attention within each UNet feature level and cross-scale attention between adjacent levels. Both will use learned, spatially-varying weight maps to compute attention scores, grounding interactions in image structure. A soft gating function, controlled by the global text embedding, will dynamically blend the attended features. We will train the system on video datasets (e.g., WebVid-10M) using standard diffusion objectives and evaluate robustness under adversarial text prompts.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, interpretable attention architecture that explicitly models spatial hierarchies for video generation, leading to more coherent and stable outputs. It will provide new insights into grounding diffusion model computations in spatial structure, advancing beyond purely sequential transformers. The resulting model will set a new benchmark for robustness in controllable video synthesis.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard self-attention or prior cross-attention works, this method explicitly disentangles intra- and cross-scale feature fusion using dedicated heads conditioned on learned, content-aware spatial maps, creating a structured, topology-aware reasoning process within the diffusion UNet.

proposals.title: Meta-Attention Networks: Learning to Adapt Attention for Few-Shot Domain Adaptation in Neural Machine Translation
proposals.motivation: Neural machine translation (NMT) models degrade significantly when applied to new domains (e.g., medical, legal) due to distributional shifts in vocabulary and style. Standard fine-tuning requires substantial in-domain data, which is often scarce. A method for rapid adaptation with minimal data is crucial for deploying robust, wide-coverage translation systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that meta-learning a function that generates parameters for a dynamic, hierarchical attention network from a few domain examples will enable an NMT model to learn not just *what* to attend to, but *how* to learn an effective attention strategy, leading to superior few-shot cross-domain adaptation.
proposals.method: We will extend a standard Transformer-based NMT model. A meta-learner (e.g., a hypernetwork or MAML variant) will take a small support set from a new domain pair and output parameters for a dynamic attention module. This module will replace the standard cross-attention, implementing a learned, hierarchical weighting over encoder layers and contextual windows. Training will use episodic meta-learning across multiple domain pairs, where each episode's task is to adapt to a held-out domain using few examples.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel 'meta-attention' mechanism that decouples the learning of general translation knowledge from domain-specific attention strategies. It will provide a new benchmark for few-shot cross-domain NMT and offer insights into how attention mechanisms can be made adaptive and sample-efficient.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning and dynamic attention exist separately, their synthesis into a meta-attention network that generates hierarchical attention parameters conditioned on a target domain sample is novel. This moves beyond adapting model weights to adapting the very structure of feature integration.

proposals.title: Layer-Attentive Diffusion: Dynamic Feature Fusion for Video Generation and Augmentation
proposals.motivation: Modern text-to-image and video diffusion models rely on fixed, often simplistic, mechanisms (e.g., simple concatenation or addition) to integrate multi-scale features from the U-Net encoder or from conditioning signals like language embeddings. This limits their ability to dynamically prioritize semantically relevant features for different generation tasks, especially in complex, multi-modal domains like video or data augmentation. Drawing inspiration from the success of soft attention in machine translation for aligning sequences, we hypothesize that a similar mechanism can significantly improve feature utilization in diffusion-based models.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a soft, learnable attention mechanism—inspired by encoder-decoder attention in neural machine translation—to dynamically weight and fuse features from different network layers or conditioning modalities will improve the fidelity, temporal coherence, and prompt adherence of diffusion models for video generation and dataset augmentation.
proposals.method: We will augment a pre-trained video diffusion model (e.g., based on a U-Net architecture) with a cross-attention module that treats features from different encoder layers as a 'source sequence' and the current denoising step's latent representation as the 'target query'. Specifically, we will adapt the key-value-query attention mechanism from Transformer-based MT. This module will be inserted at multiple denoising steps, allowing the model to learn which layer features to attend to dynamically. We will train this added module using a combination of video reconstruction loss and a language-image/video alignment loss (e.g., CLIP score), initializing from a stable diffusion or similar pre-2026 foundation model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, more flexible architectural primitive for feature fusion in diffusion models, moving beyond static skip connections. We expect to demonstrate measurable improvements in video generation metrics (e.g., FVD) and in the quality and diversity of automatically augmented vision datasets. The resulting 'Layer-Attentive Diffusion' framework will offer a principled way to integrate heterogeneous guidance signals.
proposals.novelty: While attention is common within diffusion U-Nets (self-attention) and for conditioning (cross-attention to text), applying a machine translation-style attention mechanism to dynamically re-weight *internal, multi-scale convolutional features* across the denoising process is a novel and non-obvious fusion strategy. It directly addresses the fixed feature aggregation problem in pre-2026 architectures.

proposals.title: Residual Ensemble Networks: Efficient Multi-Pathway Co-Training via Gated Feature Reuse
proposals.motivation: Deep ensembles provide superior uncertainty estimation and robustness but are prohibitively expensive to train and deploy, requiring multiple independent models. While residual networks enable stable training of very deep models, they are typically single-path. A method to efficiently train an ensemble of diverse, high-capacity sub-networks within a single, parameter-shared architecture would make ensemble benefits practical for large-scale vision and vision-language tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, ultra-deep residual network with multiple, gated output pathways can be trained end-to-end to behave as a high-performing, diverse ensemble, where adaptive feature reuse between pathways via learned gating mechanisms fosters collaborative specialization and superior aggregated predictions.
proposals.method: We will design a Residual Ensemble Network (REN) as a core FCN backbone with D blocks. From specific depths (e.g., after blocks d1, d2, d3), we branch out parallel output pathways, each a small network head. Crucially, we introduce Gated Ensemble Connections (GECs)—soft, learnable gates that compute weighted sums of features from all preceding pathway outputs and feed them as residual inputs to subsequent pathway inputs and the final aggregation layer. Training uses a combined loss: a task loss (e.g., cross-entropy) on the final aggregated output and an auxiliary diversity-promoting loss (e.g., based on predictive variance or feature orthogonality) applied to the intermediate pathway outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, parameter-efficient architecture for training deep ensembles in a single forward/backward pass, directly applicable to video understanding and diffusion-based generation tasks. It will provide a framework for analyzing feature reuse and collaborative learning between ensemble members, advancing methods for efficient model combination.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on multi-branch networks or snapshot ensembles, RENs formalize the ensemble members as integral, interacting components of a residual framework, with their collaboration dynamically learned through gated residual connections rather than being independent or cyclically reset.

proposals.title: AdvSeg: Adversarial Discriminators for Realistic and Generalizable Video Segmentation
proposals.motivation: Current video segmentation models, while accurate on benchmark datasets, often produce predictions with unrealistic, low-frequency artifacts and poor temporal coherence, limiting their robustness in real-world applications. This gap in realism stems from training with pixel-wise losses that lack a holistic, perceptual understanding of scene structure. Improving the visual plausibility of segmentation maps is crucial for downstream tasks like autonomous driving and video editing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating an adversarial discriminator network, trained to distinguish between ground-truth and predicted segmentation sequences, will enforce higher-order structural and temporal realism in the predictions, leading to improved generalization and robustness.
proposals.method: We will build upon a state-of-the-art video segmentation backbone (e.g., a Mask2Former-style transformer). A convolutional spatio-temporal discriminator network will be trained in tandem, applying an adversarial loss on the predicted segmentation probability maps. This will be combined with standard cross-entropy and dice losses. Inspired by Trevor Darrell's work on diffusion-based augmentation and LLM-grounded diffusion, we will explore using a pre-trained text-to-image diffusion model as a frozen, perceptual feature extractor to provide an additional reconstruction loss, guiding the generator towards more naturalistic outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training framework that significantly enhances the visual realism and temporal consistency of video segmentation outputs. It will provide new insights into integrating adversarial objectives with modern segmentation architectures and demonstrate measurable gains in out-of-domain generalization on challenging real-world video benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While GANs are common in image synthesis, their application to *improving* the perceptual quality of discriminative segmentation maps in video is less explored. The novelty lies in the specific adversarial formulation for sequential label maps and the potential integration of a frozen diffusion model as a perceptual prior, moving beyond traditional GAN discriminators.

proposals.title: Diffusion-Guided Self-Supervision: Exploring Context Prediction Tasks for Video Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Self-supervised learning for video often relies on a narrow set of pretext tasks like temporal ordering or inpainting, which may not fully exploit the rich spatiotemporal structure. Concurrently, diffusion models have shown remarkable generative fidelity but are under-explored as a source of supervisory signal for feature learning. This work investigates whether diverse, diffusion-generated context prediction tasks can yield more robust and generalizable video representations than established methods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a video encoder with a curriculum of diffusion-generated, semantically meaningful context prediction tasks—such as predicting future frame semantics, recovering occluded object motion, or inferring global scene context from local patches—will lead to superior transfer performance on downstream action recognition and video-language alignment benchmarks compared to encoders trained with standard inpainting or contrastive objectives.
proposals.method: We will leverage a pre-trained text-to-video diffusion model (e.g., based on architectures like Video LDM or Sora, as known pre-2026) as a "task generator." For a given video clip, the diffusion model, conditioned on partial or transformed input (e.g., temporally masked frames, spatially dropped regions, or perturbed optical flow), will generate a target for prediction. The video encoder will be trained to predict these targets—which could be latent features, residual noise, or reconstructed pixels—across multiple such stochastically sampled tasks. We will employ task-level dropout during training to force the encoder to learn a unified representation, and evaluate on standard transfer tasks including Kinetics, Something-Something v2, and MSR-VTT.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic analysis of diffusion-guided pretext tasks for self-supervised video learning, establishing a new paradigm beyond contrastive and generative inpainting methods. It will deliver a strong, general-purpose video encoder and insights into which synthetic prediction tasks best transfer to real-world video understanding.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models are used for data augmentation, using them as dynamic, multi-task supervisors for representation learning is novel. The approach moves beyond static pretext tasks by generating a distribution of context prediction challenges anchored in a powerful generative world model.

proposals.title: Unified Self-Supervised Video Representation Learning via Diffusion-Augmented Contextual Consistency
proposals.motivation: Current unsupervised video representation learning methods, like context encoders that predict missing spatiotemporal regions, often rely on a single pretext task, limiting the richness and robustness of learned features. Integrating multiple, complementary self-supervised objectives within a modern generative framework could yield more generalizable and semantically meaningful representations, crucial for downstream tasks with limited labeled video data. This is timely given the pre-2026 success of diffusion models for data generation and augmentation, and their nascent application to representation learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly training a video encoder with a context prediction objective and a diffusion-based feature consistency objective, grounded by language from LLMs, will learn more transferable and semantically structured representations than any single method alone.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-task framework where a shared video encoder is trained via: 1) a context encoder task using 3D masked autoencoding, 2) a diffusion-based reconstruction task where the encoder's features condition a video diffusion model to reconstruct corrupted inputs, and 3) a semantic grounding task using a frozen LLM (e.g., GPT-4) to generate text descriptions of video clips, enforcing alignment between the encoder's features and the LLM's text embeddings via a contrastive loss. Techniques like stochastic dropout will be used to modulate task weighting during training.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, unified framework for self-supervised video representation learning that synergizes generative and discriminative pretext tasks. It will demonstrate improved performance on downstream video understanding benchmarks (action recognition, retrieval) and provide insights into how diffusion models can be leveraged for feature learning beyond data augmentation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of a context prediction task with a conditional diffusion model acting as a learnable, structured feature decoder, further guided by LLM-derived semantic signals—a combination not explored in pre-2026 video representation learning.

proposals.title: Long-Range Context Inpainting with Cross-Modal Attention in Diffusion Models
proposals.motivation: Current diffusion-based inpainting models often struggle with complex, long-range dependencies, such as generating globally coherent structures or textures that must align with distant image regions. This limitation is pronounced in video inpainting and high-resolution image completion, where local consistency is insufficient. Enhancing the model's ability to understand and utilize far-apart contextual cues is critical for generating realistic and semantically consistent completions.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating specialized self-attention and cross-attention mechanisms, inspired by large language models, into the diffusion model's U-Net will enable more effective modeling of long-range spatial and semantic dependencies. This will lead to significant improvements in the visual coherence and structural accuracy of inpainting results, especially for large or complex missing regions.
proposals.method: We will augment the U-Net architecture of a latent diffusion model (e.g., Stable Diffusion) by inserting Transformer-based self-attention blocks at multiple resolutions, specifically after the standard convolutional layers. These blocks will operate on flattened spatial features to capture global context within the image. Furthermore, we will enhance the existing cross-attention layers used for text conditioning to also attend to features from known, distant regions of the input image, creating a 'contextual memory' for the inpainting process. Training will leverage a combination of standard image datasets and synthetic masks, with a focus on large, irregular holes to stress-test the long-range modeling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel architectural modification for diffusion models that demonstrably improves long-range coherence in image and video inpainting. It will deliver a new state-of-the-art benchmark for challenging inpainting tasks and offer insights into effectively bridging NLP-style attention mechanisms with generative vision models. The resulting model will be a powerful tool for automated dataset augmentation and video editing.
proposals.novelty: While attention is used in diffusion models for text conditioning, its dedicated application for modeling long-range *intra-image* dependencies specifically for inpainting is under-explored. The novelty lies in the targeted integration of a dual-attention system (self for global image structure, cross for referenced context) within the diffusion denoising process, moving beyond simple convolutional inductive biases.

proposals.title: Perceptual Diffusion: Enhancing Semantic Inpainting with Feature-Based Losses from Style Transfer
proposals.motivation: Current diffusion models for inpainting, while powerful, often produce results that are locally plausible but semantically inconsistent with the global context of the image. This is because standard training objectives like L2 or LPIPS on pixel/reconstruction space do not sufficiently enforce high-level semantic coherence. Improving semantic inpainting is crucial for applications in content creation, photo editing, and data augmentation for robust vision models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating feature-based perceptual losses, derived from pre-trained networks and inspired by style transfer methodologies, into the training or fine-tuning loop of a diffusion-based inpainting model will significantly improve the semantic consistency and visual quality of the generated content.
proposals.method: We will fine-tune a latent diffusion model (e.g., Stable Diffusion) for inpainting by augmenting its standard denoising score matching loss with a multi-scale perceptual loss. This loss will be computed on the feature activations of a fixed, pre-trained vision network (e.g., VGG or CLIP image encoder) for both the generated region and its surrounding context, ensuring feature compatibility. We will experiment with losses that enforce both content (feature matching) and style (Gram matrix statistics) alignment between the inpainted patch and the known image context, guiding the diffusion process at each denoising step.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel training framework for diffusion-based inpainting that produces more semantically coherent results. It will provide a systematic analysis of how different perceptual loss formulations from style transfer impact inpainting quality, offering new insights for controllable image generation. The resulting models will be evaluated on standard benchmarks and for downstream tasks like diffusion-based dataset augmentation.
proposals.novelty: While perceptual losses are common in GANs and style transfer, their integration into the iterative denoising process of diffusion models for explicit semantic inpainting guidance is novel. This approach moves beyond using these losses only for final image evaluation or post-hoc refinement, embedding semantic constraints directly into the generative process.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Task Adaptation via LLM-Grounded Diffusion and Self-Training Cycles
proposals.motivation: Current methods for adapting vision models to new tasks often rely on labeled target data or task-specific loss functions, limiting scalability and generalizability. In a pre-2026 context, the rise of powerful generative models (diffusion) and large language models (LLMs) offers a new paradigm: leveraging their rich, pre-trained knowledge as a supervisory signal for task adaptation without manual labels.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a generative model, guided by an LLM's semantic understanding, can synthesize a curriculum of training data and pseudo-labels that enables a vision model to learn a new task in a fully unsupervised manner, obviating the need for task-specific loss functions.
proposals.method: We propose a cyclic framework. First, an LLM (e.g., GPT-4) decomposes a high-level task description (e.g., "segment all vehicles") into a set of detailed text prompts. A text-to-image diffusion model (e.g., Stable Diffusion), grounded by the LLM, generates diverse synthetic image-label pairs. A target vision model is trained on this synthetic data. Its predictions on real, unlabeled target domain images are then used to fine-tune the diffusion model via a reconstruction loss, closing the domain gap. This self-training cycle between the diffusion generator and the target model continues, progressively refining task performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, fully unsupervised framework for task adaptation, removing the dependency on labeled target data and hand-crafted loss functions. It will demonstrate how generative models and LLMs can be synergistically used to create automatic, adaptive training curricula for vision models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses diffusion for dataset augmentation or LLMs for prompt engineering, the novelty lies in the closed-loop, unsupervised cycle where the generator and target model co-adapt without any ground-truth target labels, effectively using generative feedback as a task-agnostic training signal.

proposals.title: CyCADA++: Task-Agnostic Cycle-Consistent Adversarial Domain Adaptation for Dense Visual Prediction
proposals.motivation: Models for dense prediction tasks like depth estimation and instance segmentation require large, annotated real-world datasets, which are costly to acquire. While synthetic data offers a scalable alternative, the domain gap between synthetic and real imagery severely degrades model performance. Adapting models across these visual domains remains a critical, unsolved challenge for robust perception in robotics and autonomous systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the cycle-consistent adversarial adaptation (CyCADA) framework, originally validated for semantic segmentation, can be generalized as a task-agnostic method to effectively adapt models for other key dense prediction tasks, significantly closing the sim-to-real performance gap.
proposals.method: We will extend the CyCADA framework, which uses cycle-consistent GANs and task-specific semantic loss, to the tasks of monocular depth estimation and instance segmentation. For depth, we will integrate a scale-invariant depth loss during adaptation. For instance segmentation, we will incorporate a consistency loss on predicted instance embeddings and masks. The core adaptation will leverage a feature-level adversarial discriminator and pixel-level cycle-consistency loss, trained jointly with the task-specific losses on synthetic source data and unlabeled real target data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a unified, extensible adaptation framework for multiple dense visual tasks, reducing reliance on expensive real-world annotations. We will provide new state-of-the-art benchmarks for sim-to-real adaptation on depth estimation (e.g., Virtual KITTI to KITTI) and instance segmentation (e.g., SYNTHIA to Cityscapes), validated in pre-2026 experimental setups.
proposals.novelty: While CyCADA exists for segmentation, its application and necessary modifications for regression-based (depth) and instance-aware structured prediction tasks are unexplored. This moves beyond simple style transfer by explicitly preserving task-specific structural consistency during adversarial adaptation.

proposals.title: 4D-VideoDA: Temporal-Consistent Video Domain Adaptation via Dynamical Data Assimilation
proposals.motivation: Video domain adaptation is critical for deploying models trained on curated datasets to real-world, dynamic environments, but current methods often treat frames independently, leading to temporal inconsistencies like flickering or implausible motion. This undermines reliability for applications like autonomous driving and video surveillance. Drawing inspiration from geophysical fluid dynamics, where sparse observations are integrated into complex models while preserving physical laws, offers a principled framework to enforce temporal coherence during adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating video domain adaptation as a data assimilation problem, where target-domain video clips act as sparse observations, we can regularize a pre-trained source model with domain-invariant temporal constraints to produce more consistent and accurate spatiotemporal predictions.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained video model (e.g., a Video Swin Transformer) as our prior dynamical system. Inspired by 4D-Var data assimilation, we will design a novel Temporal Constraint Loss (TCL) that enforces principles like optical flow consistency, feature trajectory smoothness, and object identity conservation across frames on the model's feature representations. This loss will be combined with standard supervised or semi-supervised adaptation losses on labeled target clips. We will explore implementing these constraints via differentiable solvers or recurrent feature alignment modules.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new paradigm for temporally coherent video adaptation, moving beyond frame-level alignment.
